participant_information.name: Melissa Carter
participant_information.current_position: Family Support Worker / Parenting Group Facilitator

training_goals_and_experience.please_let_us_know:
I currently facilitate parenting groups primarily for families who are navigating multiple stressors: housing instability, co‑parenting after separation, and supporting kids with anxiety or ADHD. Many of the parents I work with are either newcomers to Canada or are parenting without extended family support nearby, so they’re often feeling isolated and overwhelmed.

I’m also a parent myself (two kids: an 8‑year‑old daughter and a 13‑year‑old son), and I find that parents really appreciate when I can share practical, real‑life examples of what works and what doesn’t in my own home. I don’t present myself as an “expert parent,” but rather as someone who is learning alongside them and bringing evidence‑based tools to the table. That style has helped build trust, but I’d like to strengthen it with a more structured, well‑tested curriculum like Active Parenting.

Most of my current programming is a patchwork of materials I’ve gathered over the years—handouts from Triple P, bits of Circle of Security concepts, and some home‑grown resources our centre has developed. This means the quality and flow of sessions can be inconsistent, especially when I’m covering for colleagues or when we have mixed‑age groups (parents of toddlers in the same room as parents of teens). I’m hoping this training will help me bring more coherence and a clear progression of skills across our series.

I’m also part of an internal working group at our centre that is revising our parent education offerings to better reflect trauma‑informed practice, cultural humility, and inclusion of LGBTQ2S+ families. I would love to be able to speak to how the Active Parenting approach aligns with these priorities and where we might need to adapt examples or language so families see themselves reflected. Any guidance on how other agencies are doing this would be very helpful.

Lastly, our centre has recently started offering more evening and online groups to accommodate working parents. I’m interested in any tips, adaptations, or success stories around delivering Active Parenting content in hybrid or fully virtual formats, especially for parents who may have limited technology access or who are joining from busy, noisy homes.
training_goals_and_experience.what_are_your_goals_for_attending_this_s:
My main goal is to deepen my understanding of the Active Parenting framework so I can confidently integrate it into the parenting programs we offer at the Downtown Vancouver Family Resource Centre. I don’t want to just follow a script; I want to really understand the underlying concepts (e.g., encouragement vs. praise, democratic parenting, natural and logical consequences) so that I can respond to parents’ real‑life questions in a way that is consistent with the model.

Specifically, I’m hoping to:

1. Become comfortable with the full curriculum and session flow:
   - Learn how the different modules build on each other so that I can explain to parents why we’re doing certain activities and where we’re going next.
   - See examples of how to open and close each session in ways that feel engaging rather than rushed or purely informational.
   - Understand how to adapt the pacing when parents arrive late, when discussions run long, or when we have to combine sessions due to holidays or low attendance.

2. Strengthen my skills in managing group dynamics:
   - I frequently encounter parents who are either very quiet (especially newcomers who are still building English confidence) or very vocal and dominating the conversation. I’d like specific language, prompts, and strategies rooted in the Active Parenting approach for balancing those dynamics without shaming anyone.
   - I’d also like ideas on how to respond when parents share practices that conflict with Canadian child protection laws or with the program’s philosophy (for example, physical punishment or harsh verbal discipline) in a way that is respectful, culturally sensitive, and still clear about safety and legal requirements.

3. Learn how to present Active Parenting concepts to diverse families:
   - Many families I work with are single‑parent households, blended families, or co‑parenting across two homes. I’d like concrete examples, scenarios, and wording that feel inclusive of those realities.
   - I also support Indigenous families and newcomer families; I’d appreciate guidance on how to invite parents to integrate the program ideas with their own cultural values and traditions, rather than feeling that they have to choose one or the other.

4. Build confidence in using the program materials and media:
   - I’d like hands‑on practice with the videos, leader’s guide, and parent workbooks so that I’m not fumbling with technology or flipping pages awkwardly during sessions.
   - I also want to better understand which pieces are essential and which can be shortened or adapted when we only have a 6‑week series instead of a longer one.

5. Prepare to mentor colleagues:
   - Our centre director has asked me to take a lead role in parenting education. Once I’m trained, I’d like to be able to support two newer staff members and a volunteer grandparent who are interested in co‑facilitating groups.
   - I’d like to leave this seminar with enough clarity to help others get started, including tips on co‑facilitation, dividing roles (e.g., who manages tech, who leads discussions), and debriefing after sessions.

Overall, my goal is to walk away feeling that I can deliver an Active Parenting group that is structured, evidence‑based, and still warm, flexible, and responsive to the messy realities of family life.
training_goals_and_experience.list_the_facilitation_skills_you_possess:
Over the past nine years working in family support, I’ve had the opportunity to facilitate a wide range of groups (young parent drop‑ins, parenting after separation, anxiety support for parents of school‑age kids, and newcomer parent orientation groups). Through that experience, I’ve developed a set of facilitation skills that I believe will serve me well as an Active Parenting trainer:

1. Strong group‑building and rapport skills:
   - I’m very intentional about the first 10–15 minutes of any group: greeting parents by name as they arrive, offering tea/coffee when in person, and doing simple check‑in questions (e.g., “What is one small win or one challenge from this week?”).
   - I normalize stress and struggle by sharing brief, honest examples from my own parenting (e.g., “My 13‑year‑old rolled his eyes at me so many times this week that I lost count, and I had to take a break in my bedroom to calm down before talking to him”). This tends to open the door for others to be more real.

2. Active listening and reflective responding:
   - I use open‑ended questions (“What was going through your mind when that happened?”), reflections (“It sounds like you felt really torn between wanting to be firm and wanting to stay connected”), and summaries to help parents feel heard.
   - I am careful not to jump into advice‑giving too quickly. I try to draw on the group’s wisdom first by asking, “Has anyone else been in a similar situation? What helped you?” before adding a framework or strategy.

3. Managing challenging dynamics respectfully:
   - I’ve had to navigate situations where one parent is very critical of others (“I would never let my kids talk to me like that”) or where side conversations are happening in another language. I’ve learned to gently redirect by acknowledging the person’s concern and then inviting the group back to the shared guidelines we created together (e.g., respect, no shaming, one person speaking at a time).
   - When a parent becomes emotional or tearful, I’m comfortable pausing the agenda, offering tissues, and checking in (“Would you like a minute, or would it help to hear from others who’ve gone through something similar?”), while still holding the group safely.

4. Clear, accessible communication:
   - I’m used to explaining concepts in plain language and using real‑life examples, role‑plays, and visuals (like simple diagrams or traffic‑light metaphors) to make ideas stick.
   - Because many of our participants are English‑language learners, I try to avoid jargon and check for understanding by asking, “How would you explain this in your own words to a friend?”

5. Organizational and time‑management skills:
   - I prepare detailed session plans with approximate timing for each activity and build in buffer time for discussion.
   - I’ve learned to prioritize the “must‑cover” concepts and be willing to let go of less critical pieces if the group needs more time to process something important.

6. Trauma‑informed and culturally humble practice:
   - I assume many parents have their own histories of trauma or involvement with child welfare, so I’m careful with language around discipline, consequences, and “good” vs. “bad” parenting.
   - I invite parents to share what is important in their culture or family tradition and then explore how Active Parenting tools might fit alongside those values.

7. Comfort with technology and hybrid delivery:
   - During the pandemic, I facilitated multiple parenting groups over Zoom and became comfortable managing breakout rooms, screen sharing, chat discussions, and troubleshooting basic tech issues.
   - I also learned how to adapt activities so they still feel interactive online (e.g., using polls, collaborative whiteboards, or simple at‑home objects for demonstrations).

These skills, combined with my genuine respect for parents and my belief that they are the experts on their own children, give me a solid foundation to be an effective Active Parenting trainer and to create a learning environment where parents feel safe, valued, and motivated to try new approaches at home.
training_goals_and_experience.which_active_parenting_programs_have_you:
I have not yet led a full Active Parenting curriculum from start to finish, which is one of the key reasons I am attending this training. However, I have:

- Sat in on two sessions of an Active Parenting 4th Edition (AP4) group run by a colleague at a partner agency (South Vancouver Family Services) to observe the style and flow.
- Used a few individual handouts and concepts (such as the difference between punishment and discipline, and the idea of encouraging vs. praising) that a colleague shared with me from the AP4 materials.

Because I have not been formally trained or certified, I have avoided describing any of my groups as “Active Parenting” groups and instead framed them as general parenting support. After completing this workshop, I hope to be able to accurately and ethically offer official Active Parenting groups, starting with AP4 for parents of children ages 5–12 and then, depending on demand, exploring the Teen program for our parents of adolescents.

So, in terms of officially leading Active Parenting programs, my honest answer is: none yet. My experience so far has been observational and informal use of a few concepts, which I am eager to formalize and deepen through this training.
